summary: This is an advice column covering four reader questions: navigating monogamy conflicts in relationships, an app helping low-income Americans access food stamps, combating sexism in academic STEM environments, and the limitations of dating site algorithms. The author provides characteristically blunt advice while weaving in observations about Silicon Valley privilege, systemic sexism in academia, and how dating algorithms ask the wrong questions. The column showcases the author's signature style of mixing personal warmth with sharp social critique.
key_points: Sexual compatibility issues in relationships tend to resolve themselves one way or another - suppressing fundamental desires is an unstable equilibrium
Silicon Valley's focus on trivial convenience apps for the wealthy contrasts sharply with apps that could genuinely help low-income people navigate bureaucratic systems
Women in male-dominated academic fields routinely have their ideas credited to male peers, requiring explicit correction and coalition-building to combat
Dating algorithms focus on superficial categorical matches rather than meaningful compatibility, asking the wrong questions entirely
Cultural practices around crediting ideas are deeply sexist and require active intervention to change
money_quotes: I think I heard him describe it as 'solving the most important problem of my life.' Which says a lot about these guys' problems.
It's one reason I like to focus on ideas rather than the so-called 'owners' of these ideas, because I know that, behind the curtain there could very well be an uncredited woman.
When we were 18 we wouldn't let 'she is allergic to dogs' be the reason we were separated from our true love. We wouldn't give two shits about dog allergies, in fact.
The shitty irrelevant data which comes out of those wrong questions get us all confused about what's important to us.
After all, what was all that progress we made in the last 50 years for if not the rights of the slutty women to go be sluts?

This is exactly what I am always explaining to people when they argue against my 'Great Men With Big Ideas' rant, whereby I complain that people who explain the history of ideas in terms of Great Men With Big Ideas are using a narrative crutch which is both sexist and inaccurate.
key_insight: Whether in dating apps, academic credit, or tech innovation, the systems we build tend to encode existing biases and ask the wrong questions, obscuring what actually matters.
